entry.title: Re: Solution Manager rises an MAXDB Log fill level error
entry.content:
Hello Alessandro,

AI was right ;0)

The reason for alert is, it was concerning that having log filling at 1.5*LogSegmentSize your system is at danger, because normally the log is filled about LogSegment level and afterwards ideally it must be saved with the log backup. If autolog backup is activated in your system, it will always trigger after filling of the LogSegment size on the log volume. So alert is concerning about that either autolog backup is out of function or you backup infrastructure is too slow. Getting LOG FULL situation means stand still.

However in many customer environments we could see, that log segment size compared to the size of the log volume is too low, therefore reaching the log volume filling of 1.5 of log segment size means no danger. But alerting is hard coding and hard meaning.

The log segment size is defined by the MaxDB parameter AutologBackupSize and expressed in the 8k pages. If it is too low and you are saving the backups not by autolog , but for example timely scheduled, it can easy happen that you reach 1.5 of log segment size filling. If the sum size of the log volumes is much higher as AutoLogBackupSize, in this case you can probably increase the AutologBackupSize parameter. However if you run log backups with autolog, it will increase the size of log backups and also increase the time gap between auto log backups, what impact the availability of data in case of restore.

If you schedule log backups and not use autolog backup, the AutoLogBackupSize will have only ´CCMS alerting effect.

Regards, Alexander

entry.title: Grouping WBS issue during MRP Run
entry.content:
Dear Experts,
 
I am writing to seek your guidance regarding Grouping WBS MRP issue we are experiencing.
 
We have created a Project WBS hierarchy consisting of a grouping WBS, [WBS-Project-XXXX], with three lower-level WBS elements: [WBS1-Project-XXXX], [WBS2-Project-XXXX], and [WBS3-Project-XXXX]. Network activities have been created for these lower-level elements, with finished goods and parameter effectivity (Model Unit values) assigned as follows:
 
- [WBS1-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 1
- [WBS2-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 2
- [WBS3-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 3
 
Our requirement is for the MRP run to generate planned orders for in-house production materials using the respective lower-level WBS, while generating purchase requisitions for externally procured materials using the grouping WBS.
 
Currently, we have observed that while purchase requisitions are correctly following the grouping WBS, the planned orders for in-house production are also being assigned to the grouping WBS instead of the intended lower-level WBS.
 
Could you please provide guidance on how to ensure that planned orders are correctly created with lower-level WBS elements? We anticipate that this resolution will also facilitate the issuance of component stock (movement type 261) from the grouping WBS to production orders at the lower level without errors.
 
Our SAP Version: S/4HANA 2023 FPS03 (On-Premise).
 
Thanks & Regards,
Ramasamy Vasantha Kumar.

entry.title: SAP Integrated Product Development What's New: Update 03/2026
entry.content:
What’s New for SAP Integrated Product Development: March 2026

Welcome to the March 2026 blog! This update introduces powerful enhancements designed to accelerate innovation, strengthen collaboration, and improve efficiency across the product lifecycle. With expanded engineering capabilities and deeper integration with SAP and partner solutions, it further enhances how teams work together—from concept through manufacturing and beyond.

Collaboration

Status–Based Workflow Automation in Configure Collaborations App

As a Business Administrator, you can now configure status-based workflow assignments that enable the system to automatically trigger predefined workflows when a collaboration reaches a specific status.

For example, when a collaboration is marked as Completed, the system can automatically initiate the workflow “Notify All Collaboration Administrators.”

The Configure Collaborations app now includes a dedicated section called Status–Workflow Automation. This feature allows you to seamlessly map collaboration statuses to corresponding workflows.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Configure Collaboration.

Product Data Integration

Design Items Explorer App Enhancement

The Design Items Explorer app has been enhanced to provide greater flexibility and usability. Users can now:

  • Assign an existing product to a design item that does not yet have a linked product
  • Download design item files directly by clicking on the file name link

These updates streamline your workflow and reduce manual steps — available now.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Details View. 

 Product Data Integration

Structure Relationships

The Design Items Explorer app now provides enhanced visibility into the structure relationships between design items:

  • Child Relationships: Display dependencies on other design items required to load the design item in the authoring system
  • Parent Relationships: Show how the design item is used across other design item

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Details View. 

Specification Management

Alternative Units of Measure for specifications

 When the Base UoM and Alternative UoM option is enabled in the Specification Types app, the system allows you to maintain multiple units of measure for a specification within the specification created using that specification type in the Manage Specifications application.

As a result:

  • The Base Unit of Measure field becomes available
  • The Alternative Unit of Measure table is displayed

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Managing Alternative UoMs for Specifications. 

Comparison of composition based on real values

Previously comparison of composition in the main view of the Compare Specifications app was based on item numbers. Now the comparison is based on the actual composition values instead.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Comparing Specifications. 

 Visualization

New cross-section settings in Viewer

You can now access enhanced cross-section controls directly from the Viewer toolbar. A new Settings option in the Cross Section dropdown allows you to customize how cross sections are displayed.

With this enhancement, you can:

  • Show or hide the outline and capping
  • Customize their colors
  • Adjust the outline width
  • Control the visibility of the editing user interface

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see User Interface Overview - Viewer. 

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

To learn more about the features of SAP Integrated Product Development, visit the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub.

You are invited to join the  SAP PLM Experts Forum  group on LinkedIn.

By the way, do you know how to provide feedback for SAP Integrated Product Development? Watch this video to find out how and send your feedback directly from the application. We always look forward to hearing from you.

entry.title: Sapphire Orlando 2026: Responsible innovation - Joule architecture for security and governance
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando 2026 is where SAP will showcase its latest AI advancements, including the next evolution of Joule. Today, I want to share insights about my session on Responsible AI —and why it matters now more than ever.

About Me
I’m part of the SAP Business AI team, where I lead work on Responsible AI. In simple terms, Responsible AI is about asking (and answering) the uncomfortable questions: What can go wrong with AI? How might it be misused? Where do we need stronger safeguards —across ethics, cybersecurity, and compliance? How to verify safety and security of AI applications? That includes topics like AI security, guidance on ethical usage, and navigating regulatory expectations—whether they’re country-specific or industry-specific. My role is to bring together experts across ethics, security, and compliance so we can build Business AI that earns trust by design.

My Session at SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026
Session title: Responsible innovation: Joule architecture for security and governance
Session link

AI is a core part of SAP’s strategy, and Sapphire is where you’ll see new innovations and experiences in Joule. Alongside that innovation, we will also talk about responsibility: how we design Joule and agentic applications so they can be used safely, governed appropriately, and protected against misuse.
Specifically, I’ll address:
- The architectural building blocks of Joule
- How they reduce common risk patterns
- How governance controls apply when AI agents are involved behind the scenes

Agent governance in business systems is not a nice-to-have feature—it is a mandatory requirement to ensure the safety of businesses and their users. We will discuss how we solve this challenge in Joule, and I look forward to learning from your experiences and ideas.

Thanks,
Sudhakar

entry.title: SAP PM Maintenance Plans – Compliance KPI Challenges for Equipment Under NR13 Inspection
entry.content:
Hello SAP PM / Asset Management community,

I would like to raise a question regarding SAP PM Maintenance Plans and regulatory compliance management.

Here in Brazil, we have a regulatory requirement called NR13, which governs the integrity management of static equipment such as pressure vessels, boilers, and piping systems. We usually control inspection due dates through standard SAP PM maintenance plans.

However, there is a practical challenge when demonstrating compliance through reports such as IP24.

In many cases, the equipment is removed from operation before the inspection due date, which means the legal requirement is actually met on time. However, the inspection, cleaning, repairs, and maintenance activities themselves can take a significant amount of time to complete. As a consequence, the maintenance plan restart date may occur after the original due date.

From a KPI and reporting perspective, this creates inconsistencies:

during the shutdown/maintenance period, the equipment may appear as overdue in SAP, even though it was properly removed from service within the compliance window.

Today, we usually justify this situation through supporting reports and operational evidence, but this requires manual interpretation and KPI adjustments.

Has anyone else in the industry faced a similar challenge?

How do you manage compliance KPIs for equipment that is intentionally out of operation awaiting inspection or repair activities?

Also, has there ever been discussion about implementing two milestone dates within SAP PM Maintenance Plans?

For example:

- Inspection/Shutdown Start Date

- Maintenance Completion / Return-to-Service Date

I believe this would allow much more consistent compliance tracking and KPI management for regulated assets.

I would appreciate hearing how other SAP PM / Asset Management users handle this scenario.

Best regards to the SAP PM / Asset Management community.

Flávio Vargas

entry.title: Migrate Data from SAP IDM to Anywhere
entry.content:
Every SAP IDM migration project eventually reaches a point where all workflows in the new IAM have been reviewed, all interfaces have been tested, and all the useful side jobs which have never been core to IAM, but which SAP IDM used to handle so effortlessly, have been moved to someone else's scope. At that point, you might ask yourself: “How do we get our actual data from SAP IDM into the new system?”

In this article, I will discuss IDM CSV Server, a data migration tool for use with SAP Identity Management which I have worked on for the last couple of months. IDM CSV Server is fully agnostic of which specific target IAM vendor or product you have opted to migrate to. To emphasize that this discussion is applicable to any migration target, I’ll use „NextGen“ from here on as a generic alias for referring to your next generation IAM solution.

DISCLAIMER: IDM CSV Server is free and open source available under an Apache License 2.0. It is not endorsed or supported by SAP. I am not an employee of SAP or affiliated with SAP in any other way.

IDM CSV Server's releases, documentation, source code and bug tracker can be found on the project's landing page at:

https://codeberg.org/freicoder/idm-server-csv

IDM CSV Server's latest release is always available for download from the the following stable link:

https://codeberg.org/freicoder/idm-server-csv/releases/latest

IDM CSV Server Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates IDM CSV Server’s architecture in the context of SAP IDM and NextGen:

 

The key point in IDM CSV Server’s architecture is that it provides a direct and secure communication path between SAP IDM and NextGen. Using any RESTful client such as the one included in IDM CSV Server’s distribution, NextGen pulls new data from SAP IDM via HTTPS whenever it needs to. Requests are initiated by the client, running on or near NextGen, and served by SAP IDM’s REST API, running on SAP NetWeaver.

In contrast to IDM CSV Server, traditional data migration schemes often require:

  • direct “database sync” connections initiated from NextGen to read from the SAP IDM Database
  • export CSV to SAP IDM runtime file system; forward to NextGen via file server or SFTP
  • ad-hoc queries from developer workstation; forward to NextGen out-of-band/manually

Unlike many of these schemes, IDM CSV Server neither requires that your SAP IDM Runtime take on a file server role, nor does it require opening firewalls for direct access from NextGen to SAP IDM Database and/or SAP IDM Runtime. All communication takes place from NextGen to SAP NetWeaver via HTTPS exclusively, which helps to maintain both SAP IDM’s and NextGen's strong security posture.

Unlike ad-hoc queries executed interactively, IDM CSV Server operations can be fully automated and require no human in the loop. This is particularly important if you need to migrate incrementally, e.g. company by company or application by application. Such scenarios may require operating SAP IDM and NextGen in parallel for a while, increasing the relevance of IDM CSV Server’s capability to automate and repeat the full data migration cycle as often as you need.

Migrate Wherever You Want

IDM CSV Server is distributed as a ZIP archive containing both server-side components to be deployed on SAP IDM and client-side components to be deployed on or near NextGen. As you may expect, running integrated into SAP IDM implies that IDM CSV Server’s server-side aims to be compatible with any database and operating system supported by SAP IDM. IDM CSV Server’s client-side, on the other hand, is a Linux shell script designed to be compatible with many Linux distributions.

“Wait, does that mean you only support NextGen running on Linux?”

Corporate IT departments in general and SAP customers in particular have an undeniable preference for Microsoft Windows. IAM vendors naturally tailor their products to this reality. So if your specific choice of NextGen makes it difficult or impractical to run Linux shell scripts, don’t worry - developing your own custom client for IDM CSV Server is pretty simple.

To make the task as easy as possible, IDM CSV Server comes with an HTTP request collection for Bruno, an open source REST API client. Bruno is a developer tool specifically designed for prototyping and testing REST APIs - very much like Postman. Using IDM CSV Server’s Bruno collection and the official Linux client as a blueprint, any IT professional - possibly equipped with some GenAI coding assistance - can build their own client in whatever language they prefer, be it PowerShell, Python, Node.js or others.

Export What You Need

“How do we make sure the CSV contains everything we need?”

Knowing which parts exactly of SAP IDM’s data must be migrated to NextGen requires in-depth understanding of your IAM scenario; there’s no single answer applicable to all. The good news is that IDM CSV Server includes plenty of content designed to make your conceptual task easier. More importantly, it does not require you to cram everything into one single CSV.

CSV Export Types

IDM CSV Server has a concept of so-called CSV Export Types, or export types for short. Each export type comes as an SAP IDM package, which can be deployed separately as needed. Export types help you organize your SAP IDM data into multiple CSV files properly, each with its own structure and purpose. The key elements of each export type are:

  1. unique name of export type
  2. database query for the input
  3. attribute mapping for the CSV output

IDM CSV Server includes a number of ready-made export types corresponding one-to-one to SAP IDM entry types, plus the special ASSIGNMENT export type:

 

CSV Export TypeSAP IDM Entry Type
PERSONMX_PERSON
ROLEMX_ROLE
PRIVILEGEMX_PRIVILEGE
ASSIGNMENTn/a

Hence, you just need to pick and choose to have all standard attributes of several standard SAP IDM entry types at your disposal with zero effort. However, it’s important to understand that export types are not limited to SAP IDM entry types. Any result set from any SQL query can be an export type, making IDM CSV Server as flexible as traditional “database sync” options.

Customization

If your SAP IDM schema contains custom attributes that you wish to migrate, all you need to do is add these custom attributes to IDM CSV Server’s existing export types. If you have custom entry types as well, creating dedicated new export types for them is equally straight-forward. You’ll need SAP IDM Developer Studio to copy packages and modify job passes, but nothing more - no custom coding in JavaScript or Java is required.

The following diagram illustrates the process of creating a custom export type -a one-time development activity- and then generating and downloading the resulting CSV data at runtime. The latter can be executed once or scheduled to run periodically in case you need to transfer updates from SAP IDM to NextGen repeatedly.

Reference Attributes Quirks

When evaluating IDM CSV Server, you’ll notice that out-of-the-box export types, such as PERSON, only contain regular attributes such as MX_FIRSTNAME and MX_LASTNAME, but no reference attributes. MXREF_MX_ROLE, MXREF_MX_PRIVILEGE and the like are all excluded from CSV, which might seem counter-intuitive at first. Knowing which identity is assigned to which roles and/or privileges is, after all, key to IAM’s core business. Everyone will need that information in NextGen, with no exceptions.

This is where the built-in export type ASSIGNMENT comes in. ASSIGNMENT is basically a one-to-one copy of SAP IDM’s database view IDMV_LINK_EXT, with all details like direct/indirect, assignment status and more included. Hence, IDM CSV Server by default suggests to migrate assignments in a format where each assignment is a single CSV row.

This is in sharp contrast to the alternative of including assignments directly in their respective entry type’s CSV, such as that of PERSON, and representing MXREF attributes as multi-value columns with single values separated by a separator character.

Having a dedicated export type ASSIGNMENT has two main benefits:

  1. Zero information loss: all assignment properties from SAP IDM remain at your disposal in NextGen
  2. Superior performance: requires constant memory and linear runtime for any data volume

As always, flexibility comes at a cost. Evaluating your migrated identities’ role assignments, for instance, requires that you import three CSV files (PERSON, ASSIGNMENT and ROLE) into NextGen and then perform a three way join

PERSON > ASSIGNMENT > ROLE

much in the same way as you would query the SAP IDM Database using an SQL join:

IDMV_ENTRY_SIMPLE > IDMV_LINK_EXT > IDMV_ENTRY_SIMPLE

With that said, there’s nothing that stops you from including reference attributes directly in your own export types or from adding them to IDM CSV Server’s out-of-the-box export types if you need to.

Each export type package comes with a switch FX_USE_FASTTRACK that you can toggle to switch from the default, SQL-based data retrieval to a more generic, identity store-based data retrieval. With the latter option, adding reference attributes is as simple as adding a new line to the load job’s attribute mapping. No changes to the database query are required.

Where to Go from Here?

If you’re an SAP IDM user and wondering whether IDM CSV Server could help you migrate to NextGen more easily, feel free to install and evaluate IDM CSV Server in your development system. Installing all parts of IDM CSV Server is extensively documented in the user manual, a must read for anyone considering to try out IDM CSV Server.

As with all software, IDM CSV Server contains bugs and I cannot rule out that you'll run into one of them when using IDM CSV Server. Should this happen, please use the project’s bug tracker on Codeberg to report your issue.

Please do not use the comment section of this post for reporting bugs. SAP Community is a great place to discuss, but it is definitely inadequate for tracking software issues and their resolutions.

What’s Next(Gen)?

This article has focused on the old world: “How do I get data out of SAP IDM?” Much more interesting, however, is the follow-up question: “How do I get it into NextGen?”

Answering the latter question will require a separate, product-specific discussion for each relevant migration target. Generic aliases like "NextGen" be gone, time to talk about specific IAM products available on the market today. Of course, no one knows every IAM vendor inside out. As for myself, I'll hence limit myself to writing about one or two of them in the foreseeable future and cover those in depth, rather than merely skim the surface of many.

I suppose that you, my dear reader, might be an expert yourself in one of those IAM solutions that aim to succeed SAP IDM. It doesn't matter whether your candidate is established or niche, on-prem or cloud, open source or proprietary. If you're interested in publishing about data migration from SAP IDM to yours with the help of IDM CSV Server - definitely get in touch! I'd be delighted to contribute and collaborate.

Let me conclude this post on a personal and thankful note: Farewell, Data Synchronisation Engine. You've been fun to work with.

entry.title: Re: Barcode scanning in “Count Physical Inventory” app –...
entry.content:
Dear @afiqhazmi_mohdrazmi7186af,

SAP Public Cloud documentation lists Create Inventory Documents Cycle Counting and Count Physical Inventory Paper-Driven Counting in the warehouse physical inventory / cycle-counting area. For barcode scanning, SAP documents the standard barcode-enabled function under Manage Physical Inventory Count / F5430. SAP Help for F5430 explicitly refers to barcode scanning for physical inventory counts with internal and external scanner devices

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/465fde923715498eb21f2efeb080ddbc.html

Question 1.

Standard barcode scanning is delivered for Manage Physical Inventory Count / F5430. The warehouse Count Physical Inventory Paper-Driven Counting app used in the cycle-counting flow is not the SAP-documented barcode-enabled counting app. Therefore a camera-based Scan button is not available there as a standard configurable function (Scan button below)

Question 2.

Barcode scanning should be performed in Manage Physical Inventory Count / F5430 using the standard barcode-scanning functionality documented for that app

Question 3.

Yes. External handheld scanners can be used as keyboard-wedge devices where the cursor is placed in an available input field. In this mode the scanner enters the scanned value like keyboard input

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/dcc5840b808f4de989a93a02ece18e1b.html

Question 4.

For Manage Physical Inventory Count / F5430, no custom development is required for the standard barcode-scanning function. For the warehouse Count Physical Inventory Paper-Driven Counting screen, camera-based scanning is not available as a standard configuration option. To add a camera-based scan button directly to that screen/process the correct approach is a custom Fiori/SAPUI5 app or side-by-side extension. sap.ndc.BarcodeScannerButton is relevant only in that custom development context, not as a function that can be enabled in the delivered standard app

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Prerequisites for Company Codes Restructuring
entry.content:
Current State Assessment

Example for As Is and To Be

  • Company Codes: 5 existing company codes representing different business units or legal entities
  • Merge Candidates: 4 company codes to be consolidated into single entity for operational efficiency and cost reduction
  • Split Requirements: 1 company code division into 2 separate entities for business focus or regulatory compliance
  • Legal Entity Mapping: Corporate structure realignment requiring new legal registrations and tax authority notifications

 

 

Company Code Prerequisites

Merge Prerequisites (A,B,C,D→1000)

The analysis for company code merge must examine organizational structures including credit control areas, business areas, functional areas, companies, segments, profit centres, controlling areas, valuation levels, plants, sales organizations, and purchasing organizations.

  • Chart of Accounts Harmonization: All merging company codes must use identical chart of accounts structure. If different charts exist, conversion is required before merge execution.
  • Currency Alignment: Local currencies must be consistent across all merging entities. Multi-currency scenarios require careful conversion and revaluation procedures
  • Fiscal Year Variants: Standardized fiscal year periods are mandatory. Different fiscal year variants prevent direct merge and require preliminary harmonization 
  • Country/Region Settings: Country keys must be evaluated for tax jurisdiction compatibility and statutory reporting requirements

 

Split Prerequisites (E→ 2000 & 3000)

  • Business units and areas Segregation: Clear operational division criteria with distinct profit centre and segment assignments for independent P&L reporting
  • Asset Separation Logic: Fixed asset allocation methodology with separate depreciation areas and valuation approaches for each new entity
  • Legal Entity Creation: New company registration, tax ID assignments, and regulatory compliance setup for the split entity
  • Financial Statement Structure: Independent balance sheet and income statement requirements with separate closing procedures

Generic Prerequisite for logistics Unit Mergers

  • The prerequisite for this scope option is that all affected Logistic Units are attached to the same company code. If this prerequisite is not fulfilled the also available scope option "Company Code Merge” can be used to accomplish this prerequisite. This can be for example necessary if all data of a company code, including the logistic data, has to be merged.
  • With the scope option “Company Code Merge” the Plants, Purchasing Organizations and Sales Organizations of the source company code will be attached to the target company code. In a second step, with the Logistic Unit Merge, the Plants, Purchasing Organizations and Sales Organizations can be merged into an existing or new organizational unit within the target company code.
  • The scope option “Logistic Unit Mergers” can be delivered together with the Scope Option “Company Code Merge” if necessary or requested
  • With the merge of logistic units, it can also come to conflicts regarding the used business processes in the source and target organizational units (currently used processes in source and target differs from the planned processes within the new organizational structure). The resolution/harmonization of such conflicts is not part of the offered service and must be done on Customer side in parallel to the technical merge

 

Sales Organization Restructuring

Merge Impact Assessment

Sales organizations assigned to merging company codes require consolidation planning with customer reassignment strategies.

Consideration

Merge Requirements

Customer Assignment

Consolidate customer portfolios under unified sales organization

Distribution Channel

Harmonize distribution channels across merged entities

Division Mapping

Align product divisions and organizational responsibilities

Sales Territory

Redesign territorial coverage and sales representative assignments

 

Split Requirements

  • Customer Portfolio Division: Define customer assignment criteria based on geographic regions, product lines, or market segments
  • Sales Territory Redefinition: Establish separate territorial boundaries with distinct sales teams and management structures
  • Pricing Procedure Alignment: Maintain consistent pricing schemas while enabling entity-specific pricing strategies

Credit Management Setup: Implement independent credit control areas with separate risk assessment and limit management

 

                                                

 

    • Merge of one or several source Sales Organization into one target Sales Organization.
    • Target Sales Organization can be a complete new one or can be one of the existing Units in the system.
    • The merge of related objects like Sales Offices, Sales Groups and Sales Districts can be offered optionally.
    • Special merge logics are used for objects like Customer Hierarchy, Material Master Admin Data, Customer Partner Functions or Sales Conditions

Purchasing Organization Prerequisites

Procurement Structure Changes

The impact on purchasing includes plant assignments, purchase organization reassignments, vendor master extensions, and purchase order handling.

  • Vendor Master Realignment: Extend existing vendors to new purchasing organizations or create separate vendor master records
  • Contract Migration Strategy: Evaluate existing purchase contracts for validity across new organizational structure and reassign as needed 
  • Purchase Info Record Updates: Update material-vendor relationships with new purchasing organization assignments and pricing conditions
  • Approval Workflow Redesign: Reconfigure authorization hierarchies and approval limits for new organizational structure
  • Merge of one or several source Purchasing Organization into one target Purchasing Organization.
  • Target Purchasing Organization can be a completely new one or can be one of the existing Units in the system.
  • The merge of related objects like Purchasing Groups can be offered optionally.
  • Special merge logics are used for objects like Material Master Admin Data, Vendor Partner Functions or Purchase Conditions

 

Plant and Location Management

Physical Asset Allocation

Plants must be reassigned to appropriate company codes as part of the restructuring process.

 

  • Plant Assignment Logic: Determine manufacturing facility ownership based on operational control and asset ownership. Merge of one or several source Plants into one target Plant. Target Plant can be a completely new one or can be one of the existing Plants in the system.
  • MRP area and valuation area: to ensure system consistency those dependent object must be considered in merge.
  • Storage Location Mapping: Define inventory ownership and establish separate valuation procedures for stock management
  • Shipping Point Configuration: Configure logistics execution points with appropriate plant assignments for delivery processing.
  • Work Centre Realignment: Allocate production resources and capacity planning across restructured entities

entry.title: How SAP Teams Are Finding T-Codes Now
entry.content:
If you've supported SAP users for any length of time, you've answered some version of this question more times than you'd like: "What's the T-code to do X again?"

It comes from new joiners, from finance people who occasionally need an MM transaction, from supply chain folks who dip into WM. And from consultants too, when we step outside our home module. SAP's transaction-code system spans tens of thousands of codes across MM, SD, FI, CO, PP, HR/HCM, PM, QM, WM, PS, Basis, ABAP, CRM, SRM, BW, and the rest. Nobody memorizes all of it, and nobody should have to.

What's changed over the last couple of years is the way teams answer that question. Not because anyone has gotten better at memorizing, but because the tooling around T-code discovery has improved. I want to share what I've seen working on a few recent projects, and what hasn't.

Why the old approaches stopped scaling

Most teams cycle through the same lookup methods, roughly in the order they adopt them.

Printed cheat sheets and PDFs. Every SAP project I've joined had one floating around. They're useful on day one and stale by month three. The moment SAP renames a transaction, splits one in two, or marks something as "do not use in S/4HANA," the cheat sheet starts misleading people.

The SAP Easy Access menu and Favorites. Complete in theory, unbrowsable in practice for anyone who doesn't already know the menu path. I've watched experienced users scroll for two minutes looking for something they could have typed in four characters, if only they remembered which four.

SE93 (Maintain Transaction Codes). Useful when you know part of the code or want to inspect the underlying program. Less useful when all you know is what the business process is called in plain English.

Internal wikis and Confluence pages. These work right up until the person who curated them leaves, or until S/4HANA introduces Fiori-app equivalents that nobody backfilled into the wiki.

Generic web search. Returns an ocean of forum posts of varying ages, half of them referencing transactions that have been deprecated or replaced. You usually find the answer. It just takes a lot of clicking for a four-character payoff.

What all of these methods share is an unspoken assumption: that the user already knows enough SAP vocabulary to translate intent into transaction code. For cross-functional users and new joiners, that assumption is exactly where the wheels come off.

What's different now

Two things changed recently that make the problem more tractable.

The first is that the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has gotten noticeably better. SAP recently relaunched it at fal.cloud.sap (the older URL fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com still works for now). You can search by classic T-code and see the corresponding Fiori app or apps, the business role, the line of business, and the product version. For S/4HANA migration work, this has saved me a lot of guessing. It's the authoritative SAP source for the ECC-to-Fiori mapping question.

The second is that AI-assisted search has reached the point where natural-language queries actually work. Vector embeddings let a phrase like "create a purchase order" land in the same semantic neighborhood as ME21N, even though the two share no keywords. LLM re-ranking then surfaces the most likely match at the top of the list with a short explanation. Together, those two pieces produce a search experience that works for people who don't already speak SAP.

I've tried a few community-built tools in this space. One I keep coming back to is TCodeAI (tcodeai.vercel.app), a free web app that lets you search either by code (MIRO) or by intent ("post a vendor invoice") and ranks the results with a short note on why each match was suggested. It also includes Fiori-app mappings alongside the classic codes, which helps during the dual-stack ECC/S/4HANA period most teams are living through. I'm not affiliated with it. I find it a useful complement to the official Fiori Apps Library when I want a faster, conversational lookup.

Other tools are moving in the same direction, including SAP's own Joule copilot work and various module-specific search add-ons. I expect the space to keep maturing.

A few caveats are worth keeping in mind for any AI-assisted T-code tool:

  • Always verify against your own system before sharing a code with end users. What's available depends on your release, your activated business functions, and your authorizations.
  • Treat AI explanations as starting points, not gospel. These tools are good at narrowing the field, less good at understanding your specific configuration.
  • Deprecated and S/4HANA-restricted transactions are a moving target. Cross-check anything critical against the SAP S/4HANA Simplification List or the Simplification Item Catalog in the SAP ONE Support Portal.

A practical workflow for 2026

If I had to describe what a healthy T-code lookup workflow looks like today, it would be roughly this:

  1. For Fiori and S/4HANA mapping questions, start with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.
  2. For "I know what I want to do but not what it's called," use whatever AI-assisted search tool fits your team's comfort level. TCodeAI is one option, but it isn't the only one.
  3. For deep validation, including release behavior, deprecation status, and replacement guidance, use the SAP S/4HANA Simplification Item Catalog and the linked SAP Notes via the SAP ONE Support Portal.
  4. Keep your team's internal cheat sheet small. Focus it on the codes specific to your customizations and Z-transactions.

The broader point is this. We've spent a long time treating T-code memorization as a kind of rite of passage in SAP. It doesn't have to be. The tooling has caught up enough that new joiners can focus on understanding the process and look up the codes the way the rest of us look up everything else.

I'd be curious to hear what others are using. If you've landed on a workflow that's cut down on "what's the T-code for...?" interruptions in your team, leave a note in the comments.

entry.title: Re: Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M): Define Integration Guidelines for Your Organi
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

We discontinued the mentioned community (and with that the email account) and to deliver the ISA-M PPT template a while ago because with more than 150 slides it reached its limit.

We decided to replace the same with a documentation for ISA-M. The documentation also includes information about how to apply the methodology - with the help of templates to sketch the scope of your integration strategy and using the Integration Assessment capability within SAP Integration Suite for turning the same into action. You can find the templates at the documentation, too.

You can also run the following mission on SAP Discovery Center to get started using Integration Assessment: SAP Discovery Center Mission - Get Started with Integration Suite, Integration Assessment. For almost every step of the methodology instructions are being provided how to apply the same with the help of a template and/or with the help of the Integration Assessment capability within SAP Integration Suite. The templates are great starting point when you are new to the methodology, and they can be used for sketching your integration strategy. Integration Assessment is a great means for turning your integration strategy into reality and for introducing integration governance across the organization.

Best regards,
Dominic

entry.title: Automate log level commands - SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction:

The interface is designed to run manually, timer-based, and through external systems.

API resource we used: /Operations/com.sap.it.op.tmn.commands.dashboard.webui.IntegrationComponentSetMplLogLevelCommand

Overview:

Managing log levels for SAP Cloud Integration artifacts becomes challenging when:

  • Multiple artifacts need log level changes simultaneously
  • Manual operations through "Manage Integration Content" are time-consuming
  • Troubleshooting scenarios require quick log level adjustments across related flows
  • Environment migrations need consistent log level configurations
  • Incident response demands rapid enabling of DEBUG/TRACE for multiple components

Limitations of manual process:

  • Navigate to each integration flow individually
  • Click through UI for each artifact
  • Risk of missing artifacts
  • No audit trail of changes
  • Time-consuming for 10+ artifacts

Common Scenarios when this can be helpful:

  1. Incident Response: Enable DEBUG/TRACE for all flows in a specific integration package
  2. Environment Migration: Apply consistent log levels across ENT/DEV/PROD
  3. Post-Deployment: Reset all flows to INFO after troubleshooting
  4. Scheduled Maintenance: Temporarily enable verbose logging during testing windows
  5. Package-Level Management: Set log levels for all flows in a business domain (e.g., all "Sales Order_*" flows)

In short: Use the API programmatically to perform bulk operations efficiently.

Log levels we majorly use:

Level

Description

When to Use

Performance Impact

NONE

No logging

Production (when monitoring not needed)

Minimal

INFO

Basic execution info

Standard production monitoring

Low

DEBUG

Detailed execution steps

Troubleshooting, development

Medium

TRACE

Full message payload & headers

Deep debugging, payload inspection

High

Integration flow design:

 

  • To run the interface both from cpi and external call (e.g. postman), we have added two main integration processes.
  • As we are passing a simple and formal payload hence converter is used in the first place after which the process call executes the local integration process.
  • In the local integration process, we are splitting the I flow ids if passed more than 1 in input.
  • After getting the I flow id, the same can be used to form the required payload which goes to the final local integration process through the process call.
  • We have configured 3 different receivers to differentiate the tenants i.e. ENT/DEV/PROD.
  • All the dynamic values are externalized nd configured in the runtime without explicitly modifying the interface.

Configuration example of http protocol.

  • We have created OAUTH credential using the token URL, Api key, Api secret from the specific tenant’s service key on BTP and the credential for all three environments i.e. ENT, DEV and PROD.
  • As the credentials are deployed in the security material on ENT tenant itself hence interface transport to the above environment is not needed.

Request example:

Header value to be passed for selecting the tenant:

Body value to be passed:

<IFLOWID>,<LOGLEVEL>

Response example:

logLevel,traceActive,traceEnabled,traceExpiresAt,iflowID

TRACE,true,true,Mon Apr 06 08:03:40 UTC 2026,BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR,NA,true,NA,MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

The response is in csv and to make it more readable we can open it in excel and the final response will look like this:

logLevel

traceActive

traceEnabled

traceExpiresAt

iflowID

TRACE

true

TRUE

Mon Apr 06 08:01:39 UTC 2026

BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR

NA

TRUE

NA

MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

Conclusion: 

The manual efforts can be reduced if it's used properly and is helpful majorly during the monitoring and troubleshooting where many of the interfaces in the same package can be put on an specific log level without explicitly touching the integration system again and again.

Important: Higher log levels (DEBUG, TRACE) generate more data and can impact system performance and storage. Use them temporarily for troubleshooting and revert to INFO or NONE for production.

Hope it helps someone someday, happy integrating 😊.

es kommt hin und wieder mal vor, dass nach der Aktualisierung der Vorgänge im Arbeitsplan (Komponentenübersicht), die neuen Vorgangsnummern nicht in die CO01 übertragen werden und dadurch ein WM Bereitstellungsfehler auftritt. Es werden die ursprünglichen Daten gezogen. Es wurde ordnungsgemäß gespeichert und der Arbeitsplan wurde auch nochmal auf die neue Änderung kontrolliert. Unsere Vermutung ist, dass es hin und wieder mal Probleme mit dem Batch Lauf gibt bzw. der Arbeitsplan nicht aktualisiert wird.  Wie gesagt, das Problem entsteht (hin u wieder) nur, wenn von der Materialdsipo FAUFS erstellt werden.

Woran könnte es noch liegen? Kennt jemand das Problem.  

Danke

entry.title: Re: Chart of Accounts in Parallel Line - Test environemen...
entry.content:
Hi @dawid90,

I hope you are doing well.

Thank you very much for all the details. We have now identified the issue.

Our customer asked us to create new G/L accounts in order to adapt the Chart of Accounts to local requirements. However, in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the Create button is no longer available in the Manage G/L Account Master Data app. As a workaround, we copied an existing account and updated the relevant information to create the required G/L accounts.

Please note that the newly created G/L accounts are now linked to a transport request and are managed as customizing.

The issue was caused by the Implementation Status field. This field is set to "N" by default, even for standard SAP-delivered G/L accounts. As a result, it blocks the transport of the Chart of Accounts G/L accounts to the next systems, such as Test and Production.

To resolve this, the Implementation Status must be changed from "N" to "P".

You can find the SAP documentation for this field here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/35e42fcad6e644c5b51f563da6dc190d.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Batch File Pickup and Dynamic ZIP Creation in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Picking Multiple Files and Creating ZIP Files in SAP Cloud Integration — A Complete Guide

Introduction  

While developing integrations in SAP Cloud Integration, file handling is one of the most common tasks encountered, and in modern integration scenarios, organizations deal with a large number of files arriving on the SFTP server that must be processed and sent to another third-party system. So, dealing with a large number of files at once can impact the system. To address this, files can be processed in batches so that additional operations can be performed and the interface runs smoothly.

This blog explains how batch file processing and ZIP file creation can be implemented in SAP Integration Suite to handle large volume of files efficiently.

Requirements:  

  • Pick 10 files from the source SFTP server
  • Combine the files into a ZIP archive and post it to another SFTP server

Condition: When the ZIP file is extracted, the original file names must remain unchanged  

This approach is especially useful for controlled batch processing, scheduled integrations, and cases that require dynamic file compression.  

 

Prerequisites — Server and Folder Setup  

Before getting started with the integration flow, ensure that the dedicated SFTP server is configured and the directories are created as shown below. 

Create the following directories on the server:  

  • inbound — Source directory where incoming files are placed   
  • tempstore — Temporary directory used to store appended batch data   
  • outbound — Target directory where final ZIP files are delivered

 

Step-by-Step Implementation  

Step 1 — Start by configuring the SFTP sender adapter to control the number of files processed during each run.  

Key Configuration  

Max Messages Per Poll = 10  (in this scenario) 

This ensures that only 10 files are picked in a single batch.  

 

 

Important Tip  

Using a scheduler with a controlled time interval is recommended.

The batch condition logic might not function as intended if the polling interval is set too frequently (for instance, every 10 seconds). Files may end up being processed individually rather than collectively.

Longer scheduling intervals guarantee that all necessary files are available before processing starts. 

In this scenario, 10 files are processed per batch. If more than 10 files are involved, a recurring condition (for example, “Every 5 min”) or even the Advanced Scheduler can be used to ensure that files are grouped and processed in consistent batches.

 

Step 2 — Store File Metadata Using Content Modifier  

After picking the files, the next step is to preserve both the filename and file content. This is necessary because once files are aggregated, their original metadata may be lost.  

Payload Structure:  

<payload>  
    <filename>${header.CamelFileName}</filename>  
    <body>  
        ${in.body}  
    </body>  
</payload>

This ensures:  

  • Filename is preserved  
  • File content is preserved  
  • Data can be safely aggregated and split later

 

Step 3 — Append All Files into a Single Storage File  

The Send step is used to write all payloads into a single file on a temporary SFTP location.  

This step uses Append Mode.  

Key Configuration  

 Append Mode = Enabled  

 

 

Step 4 — Detect Batch Completion Using Router  

A Router is used to determine whether all files in the batch have been processed.  

The router checks the following condition:  

${property.CamelBatchComplete} = 'true'

What This Means  

  • The property becomes true only after all files in the batch are processed  
  • Processing continues only when the batch is complete  
  • If not complete, the flow ends 

 

 

Step 5 — Read the Combined Data Using Poll Enrich  

Once the batch is complete, the system must retrieve the aggregated file.  

This is done using a Poll Enrich step.  

 

Note

"Before Poll Enrich, the message contains the last processed file, after using the Replace aggregation algorithm, the message is replaced with the combined batch file retrieved from temporary storage."  

The SFTP adapter should be configured accordingly. 

 

 

Step 6 — Add a Root Tag to the Combined Payload  

After retrieving the combined data, a Content Modifier is used to wrap the entire payload inside a root element.  

Payload Structure  

<data>  
${in.body}  
</data> 

Why is this required? 

  • To process the XML a single root element is required and without this, it may be difficult for the splitter to perform the split operation as the structure becomes invalid. 

 

Step 7 — Split the Combined Data into Individual Payloads  

A General Splitter is used to split the entire payload into individual messages. 

 

Step 8 — Extract Filename and Body Using Content Modifier  

With the help of this Content Modifier step, the file name and the data can be extracted from each payload (individual split message). 

Properties Created  

FileName  
DataBody  

Message Body Configuration  

${property.DataBody}  

This camel expression is used to call the file content into the message body of content modifier. 

 

 

Step 9 — Create ZIP Files Using Gather Step  

The Gather step is used to create ZIP file dynamically.  

Key Configuration  

  • Incoming Format = Any  
  • Aggregation Algorithm = ZIP  
  • File Name = ${property.FileName}  

Purpose  

  • Dynamically compress the files 
  • Original file names are preserved 

 

Step 10 — Send ZIP Files to Target SFTP Server  

This is the final step where the created ZIP file is sent to the receiver system. 

 

Scheduling Consideration (Critical) {Refer Step 1}  

The scheduling of the integration flow must be time-specific instead of running it continuously. 

Recommendation~ 

Set the scheduler in such as:  

  • At a fixed time on daily basis
  • Hourly basis 
  • At a particular event  

Why is this required? 

If the integration flow runs too frequently:

  • Files may be processed individually 
  • Files may not be picked in proper batches 
  • Aggregation logic may fail 

Scheduling ensures batch processing is controlled and efficient. 

Output / Result  

After deploying the integration flow, the system successfully processes files in controlled batches and generates a compressed output file.  

Observed Behavior  

  • 10 files are picked in a batch from the inbound folder 
  • A ZIP file is created using the Gather palette element  
  • ZIP filename: Batch20260425120549.zip  
  • Zip file is posted to receiver system 
  • When ZIP file is unzipped original file names are preserved

 

 Conclusion  

This blog post covered how to pick multiple files from SFTP using batch processing, combine them into a zip file using gather and post to another SFTP server, preserving file content and file names. 

I'd appreciate any insights, improvements or feedback on this approach. 

If you have questions about the configuration steps or want to share your experiences, please feel free to leave a comment below. 

Happy Integrating!!!

entry.title: Re: Public Cloud 2602 / Can you define a standard Company...
entry.content:
Hello @LukasKuhn,

please see below my input:

 

1) “As far as i know, if permitted, the user is able to customize his own "My Home" Page and hide or add different functions.”

→ Correct, according to SAP’s “Make it Mine” approach users can customize their own "My Home" Page. See here for more info: My Home in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal, Section “Overview”.

 


2) “I also know about the App "Launchpad Settings" and the settings that can be made there.”

→ As an administrator, during business role management you can create Launchpad Spaces and Launchpad Pages which you can assign to business roles. It is also possible to assign the same space / page to several business roles (or all of your business roles if needed). Example below:

 

3) “My question is, is it possible to define a standard layout for "My Home" for all users in the system and prohibit them from changing it ?”

→ As an administrator you can preconfigure the "My Home" Page by using UI Adaptation as described here: My Home in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal, Section “Administrator Settings”. However, my understanding is that even after UI Adaptation you cannot prohibit the users from performing subsequent, additional changes because of the above-mentioned “Make it Mine” approach.

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: From Manual Testing to AI-Assisted QA in SAP: A Practical Journey
entry.content:
Introduction

Over the last couple of years working in SAP testing, especially around SAP Analytics Cloud, I’ve seen how much effort still goes into repetitive tasks like writing test cases, debugging failures, and preparing data.

Recently, I started experimenting with AI tools like Claude to see if they could actually help in day-to-day QA work. Not in a “replace testers” way, but more like a support system.

This blog is my honest experience of where AI helped, where it didn’t, and what I learned along the way.

How I Got Here

I started with manual testing, validating features, doing regression cycles, checking dashboards, and making sure things don’t break.

Later, I moved into automation using Selenium and mobile testing. While that reduced effort, it also introduced new challenges like debugging flaky tests, handling dynamic UI changes, and maintaining scripts.

Over time, I realized a lot of my work involved repetitive thinking. That’s when I started trying AI in small, practical ways.

Where AI Helped in My Daily Work

Writing Test Scenarios

I started by giving a JIRA story or feature description to AI and asking for test scenarios.

It usually gave a good starting point with positive scenarios, negative scenarios, and a few edge cases I might have missed.

I still review and refine everything, but it saves time when starting from scratch.

Debugging Automation Failures

Automation failures can be time-consuming to analyze, especially with long logs.

I found it useful to paste errors and ask for possible causes. It often pointed to things like timing issues, stale elements, or locator problems.

It doesn’t always give the exact answer, but it helps narrow down where to look.

Generating Test Data Ideas for SAC

In SAC testing, meaningful data is important to properly validate dashboards and charts.

AI helped me think of better datasets, account hierarchies, and different data combinations like negative values or nulls.

This made my testing more realistic instead of just validating basic cases.

A Small Real Example

One practical use case where I started experimenting with AI was analyzing gaps between customer issues and internal bugs in SAC modeling.

Normally, this would mean going through SNOW tickets and JIRA bugs manually and trying to identify patterns, which can be quite time-consuming.

I tried giving both datasets to AI and asking for a comparison. It did highlight some useful patterns like low customer issues but high internal bugs, Import and Upload as a potential high-risk area, and version-related issues showing up multiple times.

That said, it wasn’t perfect. Some insights were a bit generic, and I still had to validate and refine the output to make it meaningful.

But as a starting point, it helped me quickly get a direction and reduce the initial effort. I’m still experimenting with this approach and trying to fine-tune how I use it better

Where AI Didn’t Help Much according to my understanding

Understanding Business Context

AI doesn’t fully understand SAP-specific flows, customer use cases, or OEM integrations. Sometimes the output looks correct but doesn’t fit the actual requirement.

Generic Responses

There were cases where the answers were too high level or repetitive and missed important edge cases.

Handling Complex Issues

For deeper problems like backend issues, integration failures, or data inconsistencies, AI support is limited.

Quality Depends on Input

I noticed that the results depend a lot on how clearly the problem is described. Better input gives better output.

What I Learned

AI works best as a helper, not a replacement.

It is useful when I am stuck, when I want to speed up repetitive work, or when I need a starting point.

But it still requires validation and domain understanding.

Conclusion

From my experience, AI can definitely support QA work, but it cannot replace the role of a tester.

In SAP environments like SAC, understanding the product and business context is still the most important part.

AI just helps make some parts of the process faster and easier. I’m still exploring this space, but here’s what I’ve learned so far..  🙂  hoping to come up with a part 2!

entry.title: Re: UMB operation and CKMLCP: PUP goes back to old price
entry.content:
Hello @JavierIbanez ,

what you describe is the correct behavior. You always have to differentiate between plan and actual prices.

The material standard price is a plan price with which the current inventory and the goods movements are valuated. This price can be changed via MR21 or via the release of a cost estimate (CK24, CK40N).

The PUP is the actual material price which is only calculated and available at period end. If you have a 3S material, you have both, the standard price and the PUP for the material. You influence this price through the identified variances (procurement, production and manual material debit/credits via MR22).

In the actual costing cockpit (CKMLCP) you have two options:

  1. Utilize the actual costing process to determine the actual COGM value for the materials and make the necessary adjustment postings for the key date of period end (e.g. posting to LKW account on the last day of the closing period or posting on the normal inventory account by changing the price control from S to V for the closed period ("Revaluate material" flag)). These effects will be reversed or do not exist in the next period and the standard price remains unchanged.
  2. Mark the PUP as new standard price for the next period in the last step ("Mark Prices") of CKMLCP and release these future prices (CKME) to the standard price for the next period. This change of price will persist in the next periods and all movements will be valuated with this new price after release.

To you question "Does that make sense?":

Yes it does. You have to think about it from the following perspective: actual costing determines the actual procurement/manufacturing prices for the materials. Just because you manually change the standard price of the material this does not change the previous procurement/production values of the existing inventory. In your example, the correct price for the existing inventory remains 1000 EUR for the 1000 pieces. As stated above, if you want to influence the actual price, you have to use the material debit/credit functionality, which does not change the standard price of materials (price of V-price controlled materials will change).

I hope this helps. Please mark this as the accepted answer if you are happy. Thanks.

Best regards
Marvin

entry.title: Crystal Reports Java Runtime: Fonts not correctly loaded on Linux
entry.content:
This is a follow up to https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/how-to-avoid-nullpointerexception-with-crystal-reports-java-libraries-on/qaq-p/13728522

As of SP30 of SAP Crystal Reports, version for Eclipse the option to define the font directories via SystemFontPath was removed on favor of loading /etc/fonts/fonts.conf

This new mechanism does not work as expected because:
1. fonts.conf can have an include-tag to include additional files (e.g. from conf.d) which is ignored
2. a typical font.conf seems to contain

<dir>/usr/share/fonts</dir>
<dir>/usr/X11R6/lib/X11/fonts</dir> <dir>/usr/local/share/fonts</dir>
<dir prefix="xdg">fonts</dir>
<!-- the following element will be removed in the future -->
<dir>~/.fonts</dir>

dir prefix="xdg" is handled as directory (seems wrong because it is a relative path)

Also directories are not loaded recursive - but font files are usually in subfolders

> ls -l /usr/share/fonts/ /usr/share/fonts/truetype/
/usr/share/fonts/:
insgesamt 20
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 cmap
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 cMap
drwxr-xr-x 7 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 truetype
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 type1
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 X11

/usr/share/fonts/truetype/:
insgesamt 20
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 dejavu
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 droid
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 liberation
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 msttcorefonts
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 noto

> ls -l /usr/share/fonts/truetype/msttcorefonts/*.ttf | wc -l
60

 It would be nice if this could be fixed with the next SP.

entry.title: BTP Trial CF UI5 App — All Destination Calls Failing with ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443 Since Today
entry.content:
BTP Trial CF App — All Destination Calls Failing with ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443 Since Today

Environment: BTP Trial, CF Runtime, ap21, Node.js approuter (@sap/approuter v20.8.1)

Issue:

Since today (7 May 2026) all destination calls from my CF application are failing with the following error:

 
 
Error while retrieving destination XXXX from destination service connect ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443

This affects every destination configured in my subaccount. Nothing changed on my side — no code changes, no config changes, no redeployments prior to this breaking.

What I have checked:

  • All services are correctly bound — destination, xsuaa, html5-apps-repo-runtime all present in VCAP_SERVICES
  • Destination Check Connection from BTP Cockpit passes successfully for all destinations
  • The destination URLs are reachable from the internet and from BAS terminal
  • Full MTA redeploy done multiple times — same result
  • Destinations deleted and recreated — same result
  • CF SSH is disabled on trial so cannot test egress directly from the container
  • BTP status page shows no incidents

Root cause hypothesis:

The destination service is resolving destination hostnames to 57.155.52.252 when called from inside CF trial containers, and that IP is unreachable from CF trial container egress. The destination service Check Connection passes because it runs from a different backend — but the actual approuter running in the CF container gets ECONNREFUSED when trying to reach that IP outbound on 443.

Question:

Has anyone else seen this today on ap21 trial? Is there a known network security group or routing change on BTP trial CF that would cause all destination calls to fail with ECONNREFUSED on this specific IP? Any workaround available?

entry.title: Re: Data archive wizard no disk space
entry.content:
My guess is that the data archive wizard is using temp folder (of c:) to make pre validation prior to the operation. So the message is misleading. You could try:

Create a temp folder on your F: drive (or E: drive with 180 GB free)

Right-click This PC → Properties → Advanced System Settings

Click Environment Variables

Change both TEMP and TMP variables to point to your new folder (e.g., F:\temp)

Restart SAP Business One completely

Run the Data Archive Wizard again

 

Best regards,

 

William Wachholz (https://williamwachholz.vercel.app/about)

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content:
Hello!!

Thanks for this challenge.

I have tried the event generator with Solace platform, connected it to sap CPI for publishing the event.

Using the solace PubSub+ platform as SAP AEM is powered by solace and both of them have similar UI. here are the steps Ive followed.

1. generated events.

2.  consumed them by subscribing to the below topic

Consuming only the events associated with India(IN).
QUEUE:

entry.title: Re: Custom ATP checking rule for deliveries VL01N
entry.content:
Hello @RaghavanKathamuthu 

That is another question that would merit a new thread. Still let me provide some suggestions.

First, you need to close the original sales process. The batch is faulty and it can't be delivered and invoiced. I guess you can delete the outbound delivery and reject the sales order with a dedicated rejection reason like "poor quality".

I'd suggest a completely separate SD document flow to ship restricted-use batches, i.e. new sales order type, new item types. That process needs to be to first agreed with the customer and then closely monitored and managed. You could skip ATP checks here and enter the restricted-use batch already in the sales order. This way you make sure that only this batch will be shipped. Before that I guess you would need to transfer the batch from blocked stock to unrestricted use stock. That can be done directly in MIGO. Of course, the batch will stay in restricted-use status, so it will prevent it from being assigned to other regular deliveries. That can be implemented with a batch determination and check strategy in regular deliveries.

Best regards

Dominik

entry.title: Want to become a speaker at SAP Transformation Excellence Summit 2026? (for customers only)
entry.content:
Some of you might have already been reached out by our team members. However, I wanted to extend this opportunity to our wider community. 

We are looking for customer community members who are crushing it with the agent-led toolchain and would like to share their business transformation journey with SAP LeanIX, SAP Signavio, SAP Cloud ALM, Tricentis, or Syniti on stage.

We have two events this year:

  • SAP Transformation Excellence Summit - Atlanta (September 22-24)
  • SAP Transformation Excellence Summit - Berlin (October 13-15)

If you’re interested in becoming a speaker at our events, please comment #SAPTESummit below and I will send you the link via DM for you to apply as a speaker.

entry.title: Deploy initial data from CSV on a field with @readonly annotation
entry.content:
Hi CAP community,

I am trying to update our CDS model by using @readonly annotation for one of our fields. 

Based on provided documentation Declarative Constraints | capire , I've understood that this will permit to ignore the field value on both Create and Update operation.

My question here is: will this impact deploying initial data for that field for that field using CSV file ? should I expect a regeneration of this table artifact with a different column definition when using @readonly annotation?

BR,

Tesnim

entry.title: Re: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
@rafledeganck 

It is only possible to check during run time whether an Attendance Quota is valid or not for that day. There is no check possible on the time recording profile. I would suggest having a flow to check if attendance quota is valid. If valid, execute time valuation chain and if not valid, execute another time valuation chain.
